questions.question: Explain when constructors and destructors are called for a local object inside a function.
keyPoints: Constructor called when function is entered and local object declaration is reached
Destructor called when function exits or block ends (object goes out of scope)
This follows LIFO (Last In First Out) order for multiple local objects

questions.question: What is the order of constructor and destructor execution in single inheritance? Explain with reason.
keyPoints: Constructor order: Base class constructor executes first, then derived class constructor
Destructor order: Derived class destructor executes first, then base class destructor
Reason: Base must be fully initialized before derived uses it; derived must clean up before base resources are released

questions.question: Explain the need for virtual destructors in C++ with an example.
keyPoints: Virtual destructor needed when deleting derived class object through base class pointer
Without virtual: only base destructor called causing resource leak in derived class
With virtual: both derived and base destructors called in correct order
Example: Base* ptr = new Derived(); delete ptr; // needs virtual destructor

questions.question: When and in what order are global objects and static local objects constructed and destructed?
keyPoints: Global objects: constructed before main() starts, destructed after main() ends
Static local objects: constructed on first function call, destructed when main() terminates
Construction order follows program flow and translation unit order for globals
Destructors for both run in reverse order of construction (for globals in same translation unit)

questions.question: Explain when copy constructor is invoked with three scenarios.
keyPoints: Scenario 1: Passing object by value to a function - temporary copy created
Scenario 2: Returning object by value from function - copy returned to caller
Scenario 3: Explicitly initializing one object with another: MyClass obj2 = obj1;
Scenario 4: Object copied into a container (e.g., vector.push_back(obj))

questions.question: What is the order of construction for member objects in a class? Why is the order important?
keyPoints: Member objects are constructed in the order they are declared in the class
NOT in the order of initializer list - compiler ignores initializer list order for construction
Destructors run in reverse order of construction
Order is important to ensure dependencies between members are properly initialized

questions.question: What happens to objects when dynamically allocated with 'new' but never deleted? Explain the consequences.
keyPoints: Object constructor is called at allocation but destructor is NEVER called without delete
Results in memory leak - allocated memory not returned to system
Other resources (file handles, network connections) also not released
Program may run out of memory or resources if many objects leak
Output order of construction: A constructed → B constructed → C constructed
Output order of destruction: C destroyed → B destroyed → A destroyed
Explanation: Constructors run from most base to most derived (A→B→C)
Destructors run in reverse order (C→B→A)
This follows the principle: build upward, tear downward
For dynamic allocation with base pointer, virtual destructor would be needed for proper cleanup

questions.question: Write a C++ program demonstrating the difference between using 'delete' vs 'delete[]' for arrays of objects. Explain when destructors are called in each case.
keyPoints: Program shows class with constructor and destructor
For new[] allocation, using delete causes undefined behavior - may not call all destructors
Using delete[] correctly calls destructors for all array elements in reverse order
Demonstrate: MyClass* arr = new MyClass[5]; delete[] arr; // calls all 5 destructors
Contrast with: MyClass* obj = new MyClass(); delete obj; // calls single destructor
Emphasize rule: always match new with delete, new[] with delete[]

questions.question: Explain all scenarios when a copy constructor is invoked. Write a program demonstrating at least three scenarios.
keyPoints: Scenario 1: Passing object by value to function - copy created for parameter
Scenario 2: Returning object by value from function - copy created for return value
Scenario 3: Explicit initialization: Class obj2 = obj1; or Class obj2(obj1);
Scenario 4: Inserting object into container (vector, list, etc.)
Scenario 5: When object is thrown/caught by value in exception handling
Program demonstrates all scenarios with output showing constructor and copy constructor calls

questions.question: Consider a class hierarchy: class Animal { }, class Dog : public Animal { }. Write a program that demonstrates why virtual destructor is essential when deleting derived object through base pointer. Show both correct and incorrect approaches.
keyPoints: Incorrect: Animal* ptr = new Dog(); delete ptr; // only Animal destructor called if not virtual
Correct: Make Animal destructor virtual: virtual ~Animal() { }
Then delete ptr; calls Dog destructor first, then Animal destructor
Explain that without virtual destructor, derived class resources (like Dog-specific data) are leaked
Program output should show which destructors are called in each case
Conclusion: Always make destructor virtual if class is meant to be base class with polymorphism
